title.en: Model Registry & Monitoring: Drift, Skew, Rollback

description.en: Govern model versions in the Vertex AI Model Registry and catch silent degradation with Model Monitoring: training-serving skew, prediction drift, and the rollback playbook.

story_variants.en:
## Models Rot Silently

Code fails loudly — exceptions, 500s, pagers. Models fail *politely*: they keep returning confident predictions while the world drifts away from their training data. A pricing model trained pre-inflation, a fraud model facing a new scam pattern, a churn model after a product redesign — all serve 200 OK while quietly becoming fiction. This lesson is about the two disciplines that catch it: **governance** (knowing exactly what is deployed) and **monitoring** (knowing when its inputs or outputs stop resembling what it learned).

## The Model Registry: Governance Before Observability

The **Vertex AI Model Registry** is the versioned system of record. Each model has numbered **versions** plus movable **aliases** — `default`, `champion`, `challenger`, or your own. Deployment tooling references aliases; promoting a model is moving a pointer, not editing pipelines.

Registry discipline that separates grown-up ML shops:

- Every version carries lineage (from lesson 10's pipelines) — dataset snapshot, code version, eval metrics attached before promotion.
- Aliases mean 'deploy `churn@champion`' is stable across retrains; rollback is re-pointing the alias to the previous version.
- IAM on the registry separates *who can register* (training pipelines' service account) from *who can promote to production* (release process).

> **Callout:** Rollback is a governance property, not an infrastructure trick. You can only roll back fast if yesterday's model is still registered, still deployed (at 0% traffic), and still owns a traffic-split slot — the lesson 9 pattern paying rent.

## The Three Failure Modes Monitoring Must Separate

| Failure mode | Definition | Detected by comparing |
|---|---|---|
| Training-serving skew | Serving features are distributed unlike training features | Live inputs vs training baseline |
| Prediction drift | Serving inputs change over time (world moved) | Live inputs vs earlier live window |
| Concept drift | The input-to-label relationship itself changed | Predictions vs (delayed) ground-truth labels |

Skew usually means a pipeline bug — a feature computed differently in the serving path than in training (the classic: timezone handling, or a null-filling default that differs). Drift means reality changed. Concept drift is the deepest: inputs can look identical while the correct answers changed, which is why label-lag metrics and periodic re-evaluation belong in the program even with feature monitoring in place.

**Vertex AI Model Monitoring** automates the first two: you attach a monitoring job to an endpoint (or run scheduled batch monitoring), give it a training baseline, and it compares live feature distributions against the baseline using statistical distances — commonly Jensen-Shannon divergence for numerical features and L-infinity distance for categorical ones. Cross a per-feature threshold and it alerts (email, and notably a Pub/Sub channel — lesson 5's plumbing — so alerts can open tickets or trigger retraining pipelines automatically).

> **Pitfall:** Thresholds set to 'whatever stops the noise' become thresholds that stop all signal. Tune per feature: high-cardinality categoricals drift naturally; a stable feature like `account_age_days` moving at all is news.

## The Response Playbook

A drift alert is a *symptom*; the response is a decision tree, not a reflex retrain:

1. **Triage the feature.** One feature skewed, others clean? Suspect a data pipeline change upstream — fix the pipeline, not the model.
2. **Check impact.** Drifted feature with low model importance may be a note, not an incident. Cross-reference feature attribution (Vertex Explainable AI) with the drift report.
3. **Evaluate.** Run the champion against a fresh labeled sample if labels exist. Degraded? Proceed.
4. **Mitigate.** Route traffic back to a previous version if it predates the breakage (skew case), or accelerate the retraining pipeline on fresh data (drift case). Registry aliases and endpoint splits make either a minutes-scale action.
5. **Close the loop.** Add the failure signature to monitoring baselines and CI evals so the same rot is caught earlier next time.

The distinction in step 4 matters: rollback fixes *skew* (the old model + fixed pipeline is fine) but rarely fixes *drift* (the old model knows the old world even better). Teams that reflexively roll back on drift alerts just serve older wrongness.

## Quantifying Drift Simply

Production systems use distributional distances, but the intuition survives simplification: compare a summary statistic per feature between baseline and serving windows, normalize by the baseline, and flag features whose relative change exceeds a threshold. Mean-shift ratio — `abs(serving_mean - training_mean) / training_mean` — is the honest toy version, and it's exactly what you'll implement: three features, one threshold, a verdict per feature, and the rollback decision. The arithmetic is trivial; the discipline of *baselines, thresholds, and a decision rule written before the incident* is the actual lesson.

## What You'll Build

A drift detector over training vs serving feature means: compute each feature's relative drift, compare to a 0.10 threshold, print per-feature verdicts and the resulting action. Deterministic, three lines of math — and structurally identical to the monitoring job you'll configure with real distance metrics next quarter.

description.en: Aliases are movable pointers — promotion and rollback are pointer moves, not redeployments.

left.label: Drift alert -> reflexively roll back to last month's model

right.label: Skew -> fix pipeline (rollback OK); drift -> retrain on fresh data

description.en: Rollback cures skew; it cannot cure a world that moved. Diagnose before acting.

explanation.en: Normalize by the training baseline so a +13 shift on income means more than +13 on a huge feature.
13/58 = 0.224 — well past a 0.10 threshold. Real systems swap this mean-ratio for JS divergence, same decision shape.

lines: downstream: ticket PYM-2214 opened; retrain pipeline queued

description.en: Alerts publish to Pub/Sub — the event plumbing that turns detection into response.
The response decision tree — written before the incident, not during it.

instruction.en: Build a feature drift detector. TRAIN_MEANS and SERVE_MEANS give per-feature means from the training baseline and the live serving window. Implement relative_drift(train_mean, serve_mean) = abs(serve - train) / train, and check_features(train, serve, threshold) returning a sorted list of feature names whose drift exceeds threshold (strictly greater). With threshold 0.10, print one line per feature in sorted name order: '<feature>: drift=<value> OK' or '<feature>: drift=<value> DRIFT' (value to 3 decimals), then 'drifted features: <count>', then 'action: rollback' if any feature drifted else 'action: none'. Print nothing else.

practice_challenges.expected_output:
age: drift=0.024 OK
income: drift=0.224 DRIFT
tenure: drift=0.042 OK
drifted features: 1
action: rollback

en: Compute drift per feature: abs(SERVE_MEANS[f] - TRAIN_MEANS[f]) / TRAIN_MEANS[f].
The verdict suffix depends on d > THRESHOLD; keep the drifted list from check_features for the count and action lines.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert abs(relative_drift(58.0, 71.0) - 0.2241379) < 1e-6
assert abs(relative_drift(42.0, 43.0) - 0.0238095) < 1e-6
assert check_features(TRAIN_MEANS, SERVE_MEANS, 0.10) == ["income"]
assert check_features(TRAIN_MEANS, SERVE_MEANS, 0.30) == []
assert check_features(TRAIN_MEANS, SERVE_MEANS, 0.02) == ["age", "income", "tenure"]

q.en: What distinguishes training-serving skew from prediction drift?

en: Skew affects GPUs; drift affects CPUs
Skew: serving features differ from training baseline (often a pipeline bug); drift: live inputs change over time (world moved)
Skew only happens in batch prediction

q.en: Why is 'roll back the model' often the WRONG response to genuine data drift?

en: Rollback is technically impossible on Vertex
The older model knows the old world even better — drift calls for retraining on fresh data
Drift alerts are always false positives

q.en: What makes registry aliases like champion/challenger valuable?

en: Deploy tooling references a stable pointer; promotion and rollback become pointer moves

q.en: Vertex AI Model Monitoring can route anomaly notifications to Pub/Sub. Why does that matter?

en: It turns detection into automation: alerts can open tickets or trigger retraining pipelines without a human in the loop
Pub/Sub makes the model more accurate
It is the only supported channel
